lenses.label: Practical Work
lenses.description: Projects, tasks, problem-solving, getting things done

categories.name: Memory & Continuity
categories.description: How well you hold context across sessions and after interruptions

followUps.statement: When I return to a task after a break, I know immediately where I left off.
I can pick up a conversation or project after days away without needing to reconstruct what happened.
I lose the thread of what I was doing after an interruption.
I often have to re-read old notes or retrace steps before I can continue working.
I keep mental notes about what I was doing and find them reliable.

categories.name: Focus & Tangent Tendency
categories.description: How likely you are to follow interesting threads away from the stated goal

followUps.statement: When I'm working on something, I often find myself exploring a related idea that wasn't in my original plan.
I can name projects or tasks that never got finished because something more interesting arrived.
When I get a new idea, I have a hard time setting it aside to finish what I was doing.
I stay on the original task even when an interesting new direction presents itself.
I notice when I've drifted from my original goal and can bring myself back without help.

categories.name: Overwhelm Threshold
categories.description: What triggers cognitive overload and how quickly it arrives

followUps.statement: When I have too many options in front of me, I find it hard to move forward.
Long, detailed responses make me more confused rather than more informed.
When I'm stressed, I prefer someone to just tell me what to do next rather than explain all the options.
I handle complexity well and rarely feel overwhelmed by too much information.
When a conversation gets too complicated, I disengage or stop responding.

categories.name: Communication Style
categories.description: How you prefer to receive information: format, length, directness
categories.quote: Say it in a way that works for me.

followUps.statement: I prefer bullet points over paragraphs.
I want to be told directly when something I'm planning is a bad idea.
I prefer a single recommendation over a list of options.
I read every word of a long response rather than skimming.
I prefer a conversational tone over a formal or structured one.

categories.name: Decision-Making
categories.description: How you make decisions — with options, with time, with pressure

followUps.statement: I make better decisions when I'm given time to think rather than asked on the spot.
I often make a decision and then second-guess it shortly after.
Having more information generally makes decisions easier for me.
I prefer to be pushed toward a decision rather than given more time to think.
I find it hard to commit to a plan when I feel like I haven't explored all the options.

categories.name: Completion & Closure
categories.description: How you relate to finishing things — perfectionism, good-enough, loose ends

followUps.statement: I have a hard time calling something finished when I can see ways it could be better.
I have projects that have been 80% done for a long time.
I feel a strong sense of relief when something is fully closed out.
I tend to move on to new things before the current thing is fully done.
I'm comfortable shipping something that's good enough rather than perfect.

categories.name: Self-Assessment
categories.description: How accurately you estimate your own abilities, time, and limits

followUps.statement: I often start things believing they'll take less time than they do.
I tend to assume I understand something before I fully do.
I'm more likely to underestimate my own ability than overestimate it.
When I plan a project, my estimates of time and effort are usually accurate.
I'm good at knowing when I'm out of my depth and need to ask for help.
